basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: al-Nāziʿāt — “those who tear out” — opens with a sequence of mysterious oaths: by those who tear out, by those who move briskly, by those who swim, by those who outstrip, by those who govern the affair. Who are they? The angels, says tradition, who draw souls out of bodies at the moment of death; but Qushayrī favours an inward reading: the souls of the believers swim toward God, leaving the body with ease “out of longing for Him” (lishawqihā ilā Llāh, لِشَوْقِها إلى الله). For the heart that loves, death is not a tearing-out but a swimming toward the Beloved. The sūra then turns to the trembling of the great Day, then to the story of Moses sent to Pharaoh with astonishing gentleness — “Are you willing to purify yourself?” — where Qushayrī pauses on the mystery of the heart that God turns away, and slips in a confidence about the evil counsellors who ruin kings (an allusion to his own ordeal under the vizier al-Kundurī). It culminates in the verse of the maqām: “whoever feared the standing before his Lord” — which Qushayrī reads as God's very gaze resting upon the servant, the heart of murāqaba, the heart's vigil. It closes on the brevity of earthly sojourn: on the Day they see the Hour, it will seem to them they tarried but an evening or a morning.

versets.trad: By those who tear out violently,
versets.commentaire: al-nāziʿāt (النَّازِعَات): those who tear out. gharqan (غَرْقًا): to the utmost, all the way. Qushayrī gives the image of the archer who draws his bow to the maximum, to the limit of its span: thus do the angels draw the soul out of the bodies of the deniers. Yet the oath itself is a veil: God swears by these invisible forces that loosen the soul from matter, to awaken in the listener the foreboding of his own death.

mots.sens: and by those who tear out
mots.def: nazaʿa: to tear out, pull forth.
mots.gram: active participle fem. plur., preceded by the wāw of oath

mots.def: gharq: to drown, immerse; here, to draw to the extreme.

versets.trad: By those who move briskly,
versets.commentaire: al-nāshiṭāt (النَّاشِطَات): those who move briskly, who spring forth with vigour. Qushayrī: these are the souls of the believers that leap to come out at the moment of death. Where the denier's soul resists and is torn out in pain, the believing soul bounds from its dwelling of clay like the freed beast that regains the open. The same death is a tearing-out for one, a release for the other.

mots.sens: and by those who move briskly
mots.def: nashaṭa: to spring forth, move with vigour.

versets.trad: By those who swim swiftly,
versets.commentaire: al-sābiḥāt (السَّابِحَات): those who swim. Here is Qushayrī's pearl: “these are the souls of the believers that come out with ease, out of longing for God” (lishawqihā ilā Llāh, لِشَوْقِها إلى الله). The word swim says everything: not a tearing against the current, but the supple gliding of one who lets himself be borne to the beloved shore. For the heart that longs, to die is to swim toward the One it always sought.

mots.def: sabaḥa: to swim, glide, move freely.

versets.trad: By those who outstrip swiftly,
versets.commentaire: al-sābiqāt (السَّابِقَات): those who outstrip, who take the lead. Qushayrī: the angels “outstrip toward the good and the blessing.” Spiritually, this is the law of the inner race: souls are not equal; some outstrip toward their Lord. The oath celebrates this race of longing in which one hastens toward God before death hastens toward us.

mots.def: sabaqa: to precede, outstrip, win the race.

versets.trad: and those who carry out the command!
versets.commentaire: al-mudabbirāt amran (المُدَبِّرَات أَمْرًا): those who carry out the command. Qushayrī recalls the apportioning: Gabriel descends with the revelation, Michael with rain and growth, Isrāfīl with the trumpet, the angel of death seizes the souls. The whole order of the world is borne by servants who have no will but the Will: they govern nothing of themselves, they transmit. A lesson for the servant: to be a channel, not a source.

mots.sens: then by those who carry out
mots.def: dabbara: to arrange, administer, bring an affair to completion.

versets.trad: The Day when the first convulsion strikes,
versets.commentaire: al-rājifa (الرَّاجِفَة): that which trembles violently — the earth seized by a quake, or the first blast on the trumpet. The answer to the oath is delayed: God swears by the invisible forces, then evokes the Day when all creation will tremble. Inwardly, it is the hour when the edifice of the self is shaken to its foundations.

versets.trad: followed by a second,
versets.commentaire: al-rādifa (الرَّادِفَة): that which follows, riding close behind the first. The first blast annihilates, the second resurrects. Two shocks, and between them the interval where all is suspended. Tradition reads here the two soundings of the trumpet; the heart reads the two deaths of the wayfarer — to die to self, then to be reborn in God.

mots.def: radifa: to come behind, follow immediately.

versets.trad: that Day, hearts will be seized with dread,
versets.commentaire: wājifa (وَاجِفَة): frightened, throbbing with dread, glosses Qushayrī. On the Day of unveiling, the heart can no longer hide behind appearances: it beats in the open. This dread is not punishment but lucidity — the soul at last sees itself as it is, with no veil of forgetfulness.

mots.def: wajafa: to throb, tremble with fear.

versets.trad: their eyes humbly lowered.
versets.commentaire: khāshiʿa (خَاشِعَة): lowered, humbled. The gaze that once lifted itself in pride lowers of its own accord. Khushūʿ is not imposed from without: before the Presence, the eye can only veil itself. What life had not obtained — the humility of the gaze — the evidence wrests away in a single stroke.

mots.def: khashaʿa: to lower oneself, be humbled.

versets.trad: They say: “Are we really to be restored to our former state,
versets.commentaire: al-ḥāfira (الحَافِرَة): the first state, the starting point. Qushayrī: they say this “out of disbelief,” finding it implausible to be restored to life after death. The word evokes the hoof's track (ḥāfir): to return upon one's own steps. The mind that knows only the visible cannot conceive the return; only the heart open to unveiling senses it.

mots.gram: passive participle plur. + lām of emphasis

mots.def: ḥāfira: the starting point, the initial condition.

versets.trad: even when we are decayed bones?”
versets.commentaire: nakhira (نَخِرَة): gnawed, rotten, glosses Qushayrī. They object: how could what has become hollow dust live again? The objection measures divine power by the yardstick of human weakness. Yet He who drew man from nothing is not troubled by decayed bones: to remake is easier than to make — but the disbeliever does not know it.

versets.trad: “That, then, would be a disastrous return!” they say again.
versets.commentaire: karra khāsira (كَرَّة خَاسِرَة): a losing return. Qushayrī: “a return of perdition, since it would end in the Fire.” There they are, mocking a return they believe impossible yet dread all the same. The whole sūra overturns this irony: the return is certain, and it is their denial that ruins them, not the return itself.

mots.def: khasira: to lose, be at a loss.

versets.trad: It will take but a single cry
versets.commentaire: zajra wāḥida (زَجْرَة وَاحِدَة): a single rebuke, one lone cry. All that they believe impossible holds in a single word from God. Where man deploys efforts and machines, the Real needs only a kun. Resurrection is not a matter of effort but of command: a single cry, and the whole universe answers.

mots.def: zajara: to rebuke, to cry out forcefully.

versets.trad: and at once they will be awake.
versets.commentaire: al-sāhira (السَّاهِرَة): the plain of gathering, says tradition; or, glosses Qushayrī, that which “takes away sleep and keeps awake” (from sahar, the vigil). The word names the abrupt awakening of one who slept in illusion: with a single cry, here they are pulled from the sleep of death and from the deeper sleep of heedlessness. Blessed is the one who awoke before the forced awakening.

mots.def: sāhira: land of gathering; from sahar, vigil.
mots.gram: noun preceded by the preposition bi-

versets.trad: Has the story of Moses reached you,
versets.commentaire: “Has the account of Moses reached you?” The question expects no answer: it opens a window. At the heart of a sūra on trembling, God consoles His Prophet by showing him a forerunner — Moses too confronted a boundless arrogance, and God upheld him. Another's story becomes a mirror: every wayfarer has his own Pharaoh to face, without and within.

versets.ar: إِذْ نَادَاهُ رَبُّهُ بِالْوَادِ الْمُقَدَّسِ طُوًى
versets.tr: idh nādāhu rabbuhu bi-l-wādi l-muqaddasi ṭuwā
versets.trad: when his Lord called him in the holy valley of Tuwa?
versets.commentaire: al-wādī l-muqaddas (الوادِي المُقَدَّس): the purified, blessed valley; Ṭuwā (طُوى), its name. Before the mission, the call; before the call, the sanctification of the place. The servant is sent only after being called in secret, on a ground made pure. Every true mission is born first of an intimacy — God speaks to the heart before He sends the hand.

versets.trad: “Go to Pharaoh, for he has truly rebelled,
versets.commentaire: ṭaghā (طَغَى): he has overstepped all measure, overflowed. Ṭughyān is the root-evil: to cross the limit, to take oneself for the centre. God does not send Moses against a man but against a boundlessness. And He sends him to the worst of tyrants with a gentleness the following verses will unfold — a lesson for whoever would correct another.

mots.def: ṭaghā: to overflow, become a tyrant.

versets.ar: فَقُلْ هَل لَّكَ إِلَىٰ أَن تَزَكَّىٰ
versets.tr: fa-qul hal laka ilā an tazakkā
versets.trad: and say to him: ‘Are you willing to purify yourself,
versets.commentaire: tazakkā (تَزَكَّى): to purify yourself, to grow in purity. Qushayrī marvels at the gentleness of the address: to Pharaoh himself God has Moses say not “submit!” but “would you care to purify yourself?” — an invitation, almost a plea. “What heart hears such a word and is not broken by the sweetness of these words?” And yet Pharaoh refused: proof that neither gentleness nor signs suffice once the heart has shut itself.

mots.def: literally: is there for you (an inclination) toward.
tazakkā: to purify oneself, grow in purity.

versets.trad: that I may guide you to your Lord, so that you may revere Him?’”
versets.commentaire: fa-takhshā (فَتَخْشَى): so that you may revere Him. Moses offers to guide Pharaoh to his Lord — for even the tyrant has a Lord, and the words “your Lord” are already a mercy. The khashya intended is not servile fear but the heart's reverence as it acknowledges a greatness above itself. Qushayrī hints at the bitter mystery: God deployed all this gentleness, yet in the secret of His decree had turned that heart away — “what liver knows this and does not split before the severity of this design?”

mots.sens: and that I may guide you
so that you may revere Him

versets.trad: Then he showed him the greatest sign.
versets.commentaire: al-āya al-kubrā (الآيَة الكُبْرى): the greatest sign — the hand turned white, radiant with light, says Qushayrī. The sign is offered not to coerce but to bear witness. Before the evidence, two outcomes: to open or to harden. The sign has no power over the closed heart; it only reveals what the heart already was.

mots.def: arā: to make see, to show.

versets.trad: But Pharaoh cried lies and rebelled;
versets.commentaire: kadhdhaba (كَذَّبَ): he cried lies; ʿaṣā (عَصَى): he disobeyed. Two movements: first to deny the truth, then to revolt against it. Qushayrī reports that before denying, Pharaoh consulted Hāmān, and that it was the counsellor who ruined him — “After being a lord, would you become a subject?” Denial is often born less of intelligence than of an ear given over to bad company.

mots.def: kadhdhaba: to call a liar, to deny.

versets.trad: then he turned his back in haste.
versets.commentaire: adbara yasʿā (أَدْبَرَ يَسْعَى): he turned his back, busying himself. Here he not only refuses but bestirs himself against the truth, gathering his supports. Such is the hardened heart: it does not stay neutral, it sets itself in motion to fight what disturbs it. The effort spent against the light measures the fear the light inspires.

versets.trad: He gathered men and made a proclamation,
versets.commentaire: ḥashara (حَشَرَ): he gathered; nādā (نَادَى): he proclaimed. The tyrant summons the crowd to cover his fear with numbers. Where Moses was called alone in the holy valley, Pharaoh must surround himself and shout: solitude inhabited by God is strength, a crowd gathered against God is weakness in disguise.

versets.trad: saying: “I am your supreme lord!”
versets.commentaire: anā rabbukumu l-aʿlā (أَنا رَبُّكُمُ الأَعْلى): “I am your Lord, the Most High.” The summit of ṭughyān: to call oneself God. Qushayrī reports this striking trait: Iblīs himself, hearing these words, took flight, saying: “This I cannot bear!” — “I only claimed to be better than Adam, and see what it cost me; and this one says: I am your Lord, the Most High!” When a man's claim makes the devil recoil, it has touched the bottom.

versets.trad: God then seized him with the punishment of the Hereafter and of this world.
versets.commentaire: nakāl (نَكَال): an exemplary punishment, one that serves as a warning to others. God seized him “for the last and the first” — drowning here below, the Fire beyond. The usurped greatness collapses in a single stroke. And note that the sanction comes last, after long patience and long invitation: God seizes only after holding out His hand.

mots.racine: أ خ ر / أ و ل
mots.sens: of the last and the first

versets.ar: إِنَّ فِي ذَٰلِكَ لَعِبْرَةً لِّمَن يَخْشَىٰ
versets.tr: inna fī dhālika la-ʿibratan li-man yakhshā
versets.trad: Truly, there is in this a lesson for whoever holds Him in awe.
versets.commentaire: ʿibra (عِبْرَة): lesson, from the root meaning to cross over — to pass from the visible to the invisible. Qushayrī notes that this verse is, according to several, the answer to the opening oaths: this is what God was swearing by. But the lesson is offered only “to whoever holds Him in awe”: only the heart already opened by khashya crosses through the event to read its meaning. To others, the story remains a mere anecdote.

mots.def: ʿibra: lesson, example to ponder; from ʿabara, to cross.

versets.ar: أَأَنتُمْ أَشَدُّ خَلْقًا أَمِ السَّمَاءُ ۚ بَنَاهَا
versets.tr: a-ʾantum ashaddu khalqan ami l-samāʾu banāhā
versets.trad: Were you harder to create than the heaven? He built it,
versets.commentaire: The argument is plain: you who doubt the resurrection, are you harder to create than this immense heaven? He who built the vault without toil can surely remake man. The gaze is invited to lift its eyes: all creation is a sign (āya) spread out, for whoever can pass from the work to the Maker.

versets.trad: He raised its vault and ordered it harmoniously.
versets.commentaire: sawwāhā (سَوَّاها): He made it balanced, perfect. The heaven is not a chaos but a taswiya — a setting in just proportion. This outward harmony mirrors the one God wills within: a heart “levelled,” without excess or imbalance, an inner vault raised toward the heights.

versets.trad: He darkened its night and brought out its morning light.
versets.commentaire: aghṭasha (أَغْطَشَ): He plunged into darkness; ḍuḥā (ضُحى): the brightness of morning. The same heaven bears night and dawn. God weaves the alternation of opposites — darkness and light — as He weaves within the qabḍ and the basṭ, contraction and expansion. There is no inner day without the night that came before it.

mots.def: ḍuḥā: morning, the full light of day.

versets.trad: The earth, thereafter, He spread out.
versets.commentaire: daḥā (دَحَا): He spread it, laid it out, glosses Qushayrī (“basaṭahā wa-maddahā”). After the vault, the carpet: God raises the heaven above, then spreads the earth below, a dwelling prepared for the guest. The whole universe is set up like a house before the arrival of the one who will inhabit it — the delicacy of divine hospitality.

versets.trad: He brought forth its water and its pastures.
versets.commentaire: māʾahā wa-marʿāhā (ماءَها وَمَرْعاها): its water and its pasture. God makes springs gush and greenery rise: water for thirst, grass for hunger. The earth spread out is not bare, it nourishes. And the attentive heart reads in every spring a reminder: He who quenches the body has wherewithal to quench the soul as well.

versets.trad: and the mountains He set firmly,
versets.commentaire: arsāhā (أَرْساها): He fixed them, anchored them like pegs, says Qushayrī (“awtādan li-l-arḍ”). The mountains steady the earth as pegs stretch the tent. An image of the accomplished man: rooted, motionless amid the upheavals, a peg that holds the balance around it. Inner firmness is that mountain which nothing can shake.

versets.trad: for your enjoyment and that of your cattle.
versets.commentaire: matāʿ (مَتَاع): enjoyment, provision for the journey. All this unfolding — heaven, water, pastures, mountains — is a matāʿ: not an end, but a provision for the road. The word quietly recalls that all of it is provisional: one enjoys it in passing, like the traveller his baggage, without halting or clinging to it.

versets.trad: When the great cataclysm comes,
versets.commentaire: al-ṭāmma al-kubrā (الطَّامَّة الكُبْرى): the overwhelming calamity that submerges all, glosses Qushayrī (“the supreme disaster… and it is the Resurrection”). The root evokes the flood that overflows and covers. When the great overflowing comes, nothing of the small affairs stays afloat: all that was matāʿ is engulfed, and only what one sent ahead remains.

mots.def: ṭāmma: a calamity that overflows and covers all.

versets.trad: the Day when man will remember all he strove for
versets.commentaire: yatadhakkaru (يَتَذَكَّرُ): he remembers. On the Day of the overflowing, the veil of forgetfulness falls: man sees again at once all that he ran toward. Memory becomes a mirror without indulgence. Blessed is the one who remembered in life (dhikr): he will discover nothing terrifying, for living, he will already have made the reckoning the others make only dead.

versets.trad: and the Blaze is brought into view for all who see,
versets.commentaire: burrizat (بُرِّزَت): it was made manifest, brought into the open. What was hidden behind the veil of this world appears plainly. The Blaze is not created on that Day: it is unveiled — it was already there, woven by deeds, but the eye did not see it. The Day fabricates nothing, it reveals.

mots.def: barraza: to make manifest, bring forth.

versets.trad: then, as for the one who rebelled,
versets.commentaire: man ṭaghā (مَن طَغَى): the one who overstepped. The same verb as for Pharaoh (v. 17): excess is not reserved for tyrants, it is a slope offered to each. To overstep is to cross the limit God has set — to make oneself the centre. Here is the first of the two ways that part.

versets.trad: who gave preference to the life of this world,
versets.commentaire: āthara (آثَرَ): he preferred, gave priority. Here is the spring of excess: to prefer the here-below (al-dunyā, the “nearest”) to what is higher. All evil holds in this overturned order of preferences. Spiritual rectification is wholly a re-education of choosing: to put back in its place what one loves.

versets.trad: the Blaze will be the refuge.
versets.commentaire: al-maʾwā (المَأْوى): the refuge, the dwelling where one takes shelter. A tragic irony: the one who sought refuge in the world finds, in the end, only the Fire for a dwelling. We come to inhabit what we have chosen. The preferred dunyā reveals itself to be the antechamber of the Blaze.

versets.ar: وَأَمَّا مَنْ خَافَ مَقَامَ رَبِّهِ وَنَهَى النَّفْسَ عَنِ الْهَوَىٰ
versets.tr: wa-ʾammā man khāfa maqāma rabbihi wa-nahā l-nafsa ʿani l-hawā
versets.trad: But as for the one who feared the standing before his Lord and restrained his soul from passion,
versets.commentaire: The summit-verse of the sūra. maqām rabbihi (مَقام رَبِّه): his standing before his Lord. Qushayrī gives two readings joined together: it is the hour when tomorrow he will stand at the place of the Reckoning (maḥall al-ḥisāb) — but it is above all, now, “God's turning toward him, and that He sees him”: “this is the very essence of murāqaba (المُراقَبَة), the other being the place of judgement.” To fear the standing is therefore not to tremble at a distant future, but to live under the present Gaze. And from this vigil is born the second movement: nahā l-nafsa ʿani l-hawā (نَهَى النَّفْسَ عَنِ الهَوى) — “he forbade the soul its passion,” that is, glosses Qushayrī, “he did not follow his inclination.” To see that one is seen, and thenceforth to follow the caprice no more: the whole path holds in these two gestures.

mots.sens: but as for the one who

mots.racine: ق و م / ر ب ب
mots.def: maqām: place where one stands, standing; here, to stand under the Lord's gaze.

versets.trad: it is Paradise that will be the refuge.
versets.commentaire: al-janna hiya l-maʾwā (الجَنَّة هِيَ المَأْوى): it is the Garden that is the refuge. The same word maʾwā as in the verse of the Fire: we inhabit what we have preferred. The one who made God his refuge in the heart's vigil finds its dwelling open. The Garden is not an external reward plastered onto life: it is the flowering of the murāqaba begun here.

versets.trad: They question you about the Hour: “When will it come?”
versets.commentaire: ayyāna mursāhā (أَيّانَ مُرْساها): “when its anchoring?” — when will the Hour cast anchor? A vain question from one who wants to know the date instead of preparing. Curiosity about the when turns one away from the only how that matters: how to hold myself ready. One questions the horizon so as not to look at one's heart.

mots.def: sāʿa: hour, the moment of the Resurrection.
mursā: the place or moment where the anchor is cast.

versets.trad: How could you tell of it?
versets.commentaire: “In what way are you, of all people, able to tell of it?” Qushayrī glosses: whence would this knowledge come to you, since We have not taught it to you? Even to the Prophet, the when is veiled. A lesson in measure: there are pieces of knowledge God keeps for Himself, and the servant's perfection is not to covet what is not given to him.

versets.trad: It is for your Lord to fix its term.
versets.commentaire: muntahāhā (مُنتَهاها): its term, its outcome. Qushayrī: “your Lord alone has knowledge of it.” The knowledge of the Hour goes back to its single Source. The servant does not exhaust himself scanning the future; he entrusts the term to the One who holds it, and turns all his care to the present moment, the only place where he can act.

versets.trad: You are only the warner of those who fear it.
versets.commentaire: mundhir (مُنذِر): warner. The Prophet's role is not to fix the Hour but to warn; and the warning, glosses Qushayrī, is received only by “the one who fears it and believes.” The same word falls upon all, but only the heart already opened by khashya gathers it. The warner sows; it is the soil of the heart that decides the harvest.

versets.ar: كَأَنَّهُمْ يَوْمَ يَرَوْنَهَا لَمْ يَلْبَثُوا إِلَّا عَشِيَّةً أَوْ ضُحَاهَا
versets.tr: kaʾannahum yawma yarawnahā lam yalbathū illā ʿashiyyatan aw ḍuḥāhā
versets.trad: The Day they see it, it will be as though they had tarried [in their graves] but an evening or a morning.
versets.commentaire: The closing verse, of striking gentleness. Qushayrī: “the Day they see the Resurrection, it will seem to them they tarried but an evening or a morning — so immense is what they see that the length of their sojourn beneath the earth shrinks in their eyes.” The whole earthly span, measured against the unveiled eternity, contracts into a dusk, into a dawn. The sūra opened on the souls that swim toward God closes on this truth: what we believe long is but an instant — hence the urgency, not of knowing the when, but of swimming now toward the shore.

mots.gram: jussive imperfect verb (negation of the past)

mots.def: ʿashiyya: evening, the close of day.
